```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
names("Clara"; "Fannie"; "Hannah"; "Kara").
points(181; 184; 187; 190).
orders(2; 4; 6; 7).

% Each dancer has exactly one point value and one performance order.
dancer(N, P, O) :- names(N), points(P), orders(O), #count{X : dancer(X, P, O)} = 1.

% Ensure uniqueness: no two dancers share the same name, points, or order.
:- dancer(N1, P1, O1), dancer(N2, P2, O2), N1 != N2, N1 == N2.
:- dancer(N1, P1, O1), dancer(N2, P2, O2), P1 != P2, P1 == P2.
:- dancer(N1, P1, O1), dancer(N2, P2, O2), O1 != O2, O1 == O2.

% 1. The person who danced seventh scored somewhat higher than the dancer who performed fourth.
:- dancer(_, P7, 7), dancer(_, P4, 4), P7 <= P4.

% 2. The dancer who performed second scored 3 points lower than the dancer who performed fourth.
:- dancer(_, P2, 2), dancer(_, P4, 4), P2 != P4 - 3.

% 3. Kara scored somewhat lower than Fannie.
:- dancer("Kara", PK, _), dancer("Fannie", PF, _), PK >= PF.

% 4. Hannah was either the dancer who performed fourth or the person who danced sixth.
:- dancer("Hannah", _, O), O != 4, O != 6.

% 5.1 Clara and Kara are different.
:- dancer("Clara", _, _), dancer("Kara", _, _), "Clara" == "Kara".

% 5.2 Clara scored 184 points or danced seventh.
:- dancer("Clara", P, O), P != 184, O != 7.

% 5.3 Kara scored 184 points or danced seventh.
:- dancer("Kara", P, O), P != 184, O != 7.

% Ensure all values are assigned exactly once.
:- #count{N : dancer(N, _, _)} != 4.
:- #count{P : dancer(_, P, _)} != 4.
:- #count{O : dancer(_, _, O)} != 4.
```

```asp
:- #count{I, Y, C : discover(I, Y, C)} != 4.
:- #count{I, Y, C : discover(I, Y, C)} != 4.
:- #count{I, Y, C : discover(I, Y, C)} != 4.

:- #count{Y : discover(I, Y, C)} > 1, islands(I).
:- #count{C : discover(I, Y, C)} > 1, culture(C).

% Clue 1: Jujihm was discovered in 1768.
discover("Jujihm", 1768, C) :- culture(C).

% Clue 2: Verinya was discovered in 1761.
discover("Verinya", 1761, C) :- culture(C).

% Clue 3: Jujihm was discovered 14 years after the island on which the Wainani people lived.
% So Wainani lived on the island discovered in 1754.
discover(I, 1754, "Wainani") :- islands(I).

% Clue 4: The island on which the Kukani people lived was discovered 7 years after Fushil.

% But 1761 is Verinya → so Verinya has culture Kukani
discover("Fushil", 1754, C) :- culture(C).
discover("Verinya", 1761, "Kukani").

% Clue 5: The island discovered in 1768 is either the island on which the Hakili people lived or is Fushil.
% But Fushil is 1754 → so it must be Hakili
discover("Jujihm", 1768, "Hakili").
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
presenters("Alexander"; "Gerard"; "Inez"; "Mable").
days("May 10th"; "May 11th"; "May 12th"; "May 13th").
topics("global warming"; "nitrogen usage"; "sulfur oxide"; "wind power").

% Each presenter gives exactly one talk on one day and on one topic.
1 { talk(P, D, T) : days(D), topics(T) } 1 :- presenters(P).

% Each day has exactly one talk.
1 { talk(P, D, T) : presenters(P), topics(T) } 1 :- days(D).

% Each topic is used exactly once.
1 { talk(P, D, T) : presenters(P), days(D) } 1 :- topics(T).

% 1. The sulfur oxide expert is scheduled 1 day after Gerard.
:- talk("Gerard", D1, _), talk(_, D2, "sulfur oxide"), D2 != D1+1.

% 2. The nitrogen usage expert is scheduled 1 day before Alexander.
:- talk(_, D1, "nitrogen usage"), talk("Alexander", D2, _), D2 != D1+1.

% 3. Inez will discuss global warming.
:- talk("Inez", _, T), T != "global warming".

% 4. Mable is scheduled 2 days after the sulfur oxide expert.
:- talk(_, D1, "sulfur oxide"), talk("Mable", D2, _), D2 != D1+2.

% Define day ordering: May 10th < May 11th < May 12th < May 13th
% Use a helper predicate to express day ordering
day_after(D1, D2) :- days(D1), days(D2), D1="May 10th", D2="May 11th".
day_after(D1, D2) :- days(D1), days(D2), D1="May 11th", D2="May 12th".
day_after(D1, D2) :- days(D1), days(D2), D1="May 12th", D2="May 13th".

% Use the day_after relation to express the constraints more safely
% Constraint 1: sulfur oxide expert is 1 day after Gerard
:- talk("Gerard", D1, _), talk(_, D2, "sulfur oxide"), not day_after(D1, D2).

% Constraint 2: nitrogen usage expert is 1 day before Alexander
:- talk(_, D1, "nitrogen usage"), talk("Alexander", D2, _), not day_after(D1, D2).

% Constraint 4: Mable is 2 days after sulfur oxide expert
% D2 = D1 + 2: only possible if D1 = May 10th, D2 = May 12th or D1 = May 11th, D2 = May 13th
:- talk(_, D1, "sulfur oxide"), talk("Mable", D2, _), not (D1="May 10th", D2="May 12th"); not (D1="May 11th", D2="May 13th").
```

```asp
% Define the constants
diplomats("Gilbert"; "Macdonald"; "Pickett"; "Vasquez").
months(1; 2; 3; 4).
duration(4; 5; 9; 10).

% Each diplomat is assigned exactly one month and one duration
assign(D, M, Dur) :- diplomats(D), months(M), duration(Dur), 
    #count { D2, M2, Dur2 : assign(D2, M2, Dur2), D2 = D } = 1.

% Each month is used exactly once
:- #count { D, Dur : assign(D, M, Dur) } != 1, months(M).

% Each duration is used exactly once
:- #count { D, M : assign(D, M, Dur) } != 1, duration(Dur).

% 1. Vasquez leaves after Macdonald
:- assign("Vasquez", M1, _), assign("Macdonald", M2, _), M1 <= M2.

% 2. Vasquez leaves 1 month before the ambassador with the 5-day visit
:- assign("Vasquez", M1, _), assign(_, M2, 5), M1 != M2 - 1.

% 3.1 Gilbert and the 4-day ambassador are different
:- assign("Gilbert", _, _), assign(_, _, 4), "Gilbert" = _.

% 3.2 Gilbert is in January OR is the 4-day ambassador
:- assign("Gilbert", M, Dur), M != 1, Dur != 4.

% 3.3 The 4-day ambassador is in January OR is the 4-day ambassador
% This is always true, so no constraint needed — tautology.

% 4. Macdonald leaves 1 month before the 4-day ambassador
:- assign("Macdonald", M1, _), assign(_, M2, 4), M1 != M2 - 1.

% 5. 4-day ambassador leaves before 9-day ambassador
:- assign(_, M1, 4), assign(_, M2, 9), M1 >= M2.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
games("Angry Ants"; "Bubble Bombs"; "Flowcarts"; "Peter Parka"; "Wizard World").
months(3; 4; 5; 6; 7).
developers("Apptastic"; "Digibits"; "Gadingo"; "Novio"; "Vortia").

% Each game is released in exactly one month and by exactly one developer.
1 {released(G, M, D) : months(M), developers(D)} 1 :- games(G).

% Each month has exactly one game.
1 {released(G, M, D) : games(G), developers(D)} 1 :- months(M).

% Each developer has exactly one game.
1 {released(G, M, D) : games(G), months(M)} 1 :- developers(D).

% 1. The app released by Vortia was released 2 months later than Flowcarts.
:- released("Flowcarts", M1, "Vortia"), released(G2, M2, D2), M2 = M1 + 2, G2 = "Flowcarts", D2 = "Vortia".

% 2. Angry Ants was released 3 months later than the app released by Gadingo.
:- released(G1, M1, "Gadingo"), released("Angry Ants", M2, D2), M2 = M1 + 3.

% 3. The game released by Apptastic was released 3 months later than Flowcarts.
:- released("Flowcarts", M1, D1), released(G2, M2, "Apptastic"), M2 = M1 + 3.

% 4.1 The game released by Novio and Bubble Bombs are different.
:- released("Bubble Bombs", M, "Novio").

% 4.2 The game released by Novio is the game released in July or Bubble Bombs.
:- released(G, M, "Novio"), M != 7, G != "Bubble Bombs".

% 4.3 The game released in July is the game released by Novio or Bubble Bombs.
:- released(G, 7, D), G != "Bubble Bombs", D != "Novio".

% 5.1 Peter Parka and the game released in May are different.
:- released("Peter Parka", M, D), M = 5.

% 5.2 Peter Parka is the app released by Novio or the game released in May.
:- released("Peter Parka", M, D), D != "Novio", M != 5.

% 5.3 The game released in May is the app released by Novio or Peter Parka.
:- released(G, 5, D), D != "Novio", G != "Peter Parka".

% 6. The game released in July was made by Apptastic.
:- released(G, 7, D), D != "Apptastic".
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
witnesses("Benny Baron"; "Dan Delgado"; "Edith Estes"; "Gil Gates").
dates(4; 5; 6; 7).
towns("Crescent City"; "Embden"; "Islesboro"; "Walnut Creek").

% Each witness, date, and town appears exactly once.
% One-to-one mapping: each witness has one date and one town.
1 {sighting(W, D, T) : dates(D), towns(T)} 1 :- witnesses(W).
1 {sighting(W, D, T) : witnesses(W), towns(T)} 1 :- dates(D).
1 {sighting(W, D, T) : witnesses(W), dates(D)} 1 :- towns(T).

% 1. Benny Baron's sighting was submitted 2 days after Dan Delgado's report.
:- sighting("Benny Baron", D1, _), sighting("Dan Delgado", D2, _), D1 != D2 + 2.

% 2.2 The August 7 report was reported by Gil Gates or was from Walnut Creek.
:- sighting(W, 7, T), W != "Gil Gates", T != "Walnut Creek".

% 2.3 The August 5 sighting was reported by Gil Gates or was from Walnut Creek.
:- sighting(W, 5, T), W != "Gil Gates", T != "Walnut Creek".

% 3. The report from Crescent City was submitted 1 day after the sighting from Islesboro.
:- sighting(_, D1, "Crescent City"), sighting(_, D2, "Islesboro"), D1 != D2 + 1.

% 4. The sighting from Walnut Creek was the August 7 report or Edith Estes's sighting.
:- sighting(W, D, "Walnut Creek"), D != 7, W != "Edith Estes".
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
waterfall("Sihat"; "Rhoqua"; "Nyalt"; "Inawatai").
height(100; 105; 110; 115).
country("Brazil"; "Nigeria"; "Tibet"; "Switzerland").

% Each waterfall is associated with exactly one height and one country.
1 {match(W, H, C) : height(H), country(C)} 1 :- waterfall(W).

% Each height is used exactly once.
1 {match(W, H, C) : waterfall(W), country(C)} 1 :- height(H).

% Each country is used exactly once.
1 {match(W, H, C) : waterfall(W), height(H)} 1 :- country(C).

% 1. The 115 ft tall waterfall is either the waterfall in Nigeria or the waterfall located in Tibet.
:- height(H), H = 115, not (match(_, H, "Nigeria") ; match(_, H, "Tibet")).

% 2. Inawatai is 100 ft tall.
:- waterfall(W), W = "Inawatai", not match(W, 100, _).

% 3. Nyalt is 10 ft taller than Rhoqua.
:- waterfall(W1), W1 = "Nyalt", waterfall(W2), W2 = "Rhoqua",
   not (match(W1, H1, _), match(W2, H2, _), H1 = H2 + 10).

% 4. The waterfall in Brazil is somewhat shorter than Rhoqua.
:- country(C), C = "Brazil", waterfall(W2), W2 = "Rhoqua",
   not (match(_, H1, C), match(_, H2, _), H1 < H2).

% 5. The waterfall in Brazil is 10 ft shorter than the waterfall located in Nigeria.
:- country(C1), C1 = "Brazil", country(C2), C2 = "Nigeria",
   not (match(_, H1, C1), match(_, H2, C2), H1 = H2 - 10).
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
players("Awad"; "Daregh"; "Gardelli"; "Rothvum").
years(1976; 1979; 1982; 1985).
positions("center back"; "center forward"; "goalie"; "sweeper").

% Each year is used by exactly one player.
1 { plays(Player, Year, Position) : players(Player), positions(Position) } 1 :- years(Year).

% Each position is used by exactly one player.

% 1. Daregh started 6 years before the goalie.
:- plays("Daregh", Y1, _), plays(_, Y2, "goalie"), Y2 - Y1 != 6.

% 2.1 The player who started in 1982 and Daregh are different.
:- plays("Daregh", 1982, _).

% 2.2 The player who started in 1982 was the goalie or Daregh.
:- plays(P, 1982, Pos), P != "Daregh", Pos != "goalie".

% 2.3 Daregh was the goalie or the player who started in 1982.
:- plays("Daregh", Y, Pos), Y != 1982, Pos != "goalie".

% 3. Awad began playing in 1985.
:- plays("Awad", Y, _), Y != 1985.

% 4. The player who started in 1976 was the sweeper.
:- plays(P, 1976, Pos), Pos != "sweeper".

% 5.1 Gardelli and the player who started in 1979 are different.
:- plays("Gardelli", 1979, _).

% 5.2 Gardelli was the center back or the player who started in 1979.
:- plays("Gardelli", Y, Pos), Y != 1979, Pos != "center back".

% 5.3 The player who started in 1979 was the center back or Gardelli.
:- plays(P, 1979, Pos), P != "Gardelli", Pos != "center back".
```

```asp
% Define the constants
names("Delia"; "Hope"; "Patricia"; "Wayne").
price(5; 6; 7; 8).
drink("cafe americano"; "cafe latte"; "cappuccino"; "chai tea").

% Each person has exactly one drink and one price
{order(Name, Drink, Price)} :- names(Name), drink(Drink), price(Price).

% Each drink is used exactly once
:- #count{N, D, P : order(N, D, P)} != 1, drink(D).

% Each price is used exactly once
:- #count{N, D, P : order(N, D, P)} != 1, price(P).

% 1. Hope paid less than Delia
:- order("Hope", _, P1), order("Delia", _, P2), P1 >= P2.

% 2. Patricia had the cappuccino
order("Patricia", "cappuccino", P) :- price(P).

% 3. The one who had the cafe americano paid 1 dollar more than Patricia
:- order(N1, "cafe americano", P1), order("Patricia", "cappuccino", P2), P1 != P2 + 1.

% 4. Hope paid more than the one who had the cafe americano
:- order("Hope", _, P1), order(N2, "cafe americano", P2), P1 <= P2.

% 5. The one who had the chai tea paid 1 dollar more than the one who had the cafe americano
:- order(N1, "chai tea", P1), order(N2, "cafe americano", P2), P1 != P2 + 1.

% Ensure all variables are grounded by enforcing full assignment
% We use the fact that each name, drink, and price must appear exactly once
% The above constraints already enforce uniqueness, but we add explicit grounding
% via a minimal model to avoid non-grounded variables.
% We use the fact that the only way to satisfy the constraints is to have one full assignment.
% The above constraints are sufficient and safe now.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
craters("Cersay"; "Moriwa"; "Ormagh"; "Vorckin").
diameter(100; 125; 150; 175).
period("Jurassic"; "Ordovician"; "Permian"; "Triassic").

% Each crater has exactly one diameter and one period.
{crater_info(C, D, P) : diameter(D), period(P)} = 1 :- craters(C).

% No duplicate values in any category.
:- craters(C1), craters(C2), C1 != C2, crater_info(C1, D, P1), crater_info(C2, D, P2).
:- craters(C1), craters(C2), C1 != C2, crater_info(C1, D1, P), crater_info(C2, D2, P).
:- period(P1), period(P2), P1 != P2, crater_info(C, D, P1), crater_info(C, D, P2).

% 1. The Vorckin impact crater is 125 meters wide.
crater_info("Vorckin", 125, P) :- period(P).

% 2.1 The 150 meters wide one and the Ordovician impact crater are different.
:- crater_info(C, 150, P1), crater_info(C, D, "Ordovician").

% 2.2 The 150 meters wide one is either the Ordovician impact crater or the Permian impact crater.
:- crater_info(C, 150, P), P != "Ordovician", P != "Permian".

% 2.3 The Ordovician impact crater is either the Ordovician impact crater or the Permian impact crater.
% This is tautological — always true, so no constraint needed.

% 3. The Ordovician impact crater is 25 meters wider than the Jurassic impact crater.
:- crater_info(C1, D1, "Ordovician"), crater_info(C2, D2, "Jurassic"), D1 != D2 + 25.

% 4.1 The Triassic impact crater and the Ormagh impact crater are different.
:- crater_info("Ormagh", D1, P1), crater_info("Ormagh", D2, "Triassic").

% 4.2 The Triassic impact crater is either the Ormagh impact crater or the 150 meters wide one.
:- crater_info(C, D, "Triassic"), C != "Ormagh", D != 150.

% 4.3 The Ormagh impact crater is either the Ormagh impact crater or the 150 meters wide one.
:- crater_info("Ormagh", D, P), D != 150.

% 5. The Cersay impact crater was formed during the Jurassic period.
crater_info("Cersay", D, "Jurassic") :- diameter(D).
```

```asp
:- finish(_, T1, "maroon"), finish("Salvador", T2, _), T1 = T2 - 1.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.

ages(109; 110; 111; 112).
states("Kansas"; "Louisiana"; "Pennsylvania"; "South Dakota").

% Each age is used exactly once.

:- #count{Age, State : match(Name, Age, State)} != 1, names(Name).

:- match("Kyle", A1, _), match("Arlene", A2, _), A1 != A2 + 1.

% 2. Kyle, the 109-year-old, and the 110-year-old are all different people.
% So Kyle ≠ 109, Kyle ≠ 110 → Kyle must be 111 or 112.
:- match("Kyle", 109, _).
:- match("Kyle", 110, _).

% 3. The Pennsylvania native is older than Ernesto.

% 4. Ernesto is older than the Louisiana native.
:- match("Ernesto", A1, _), match(_, A2, "Louisiana"), A1 <= A2.

% 5. The person who is 111 years old is a native of South Dakota.
:- match(_, 111, S), S != "South Dakota".
```

```asp
:- work("Peter", Sec, Riders), Sec != "blue", Riders != 125.
```

```asp
day_after(D1, D2) :- D1 = "May 12th", D2 = "May 13th".
```

```asp
% Final constraint: the 7th lot must be sold for exactly 20 less than Daniel's purchase
```

```asp
% Each ranking is used exactly once.

% 4.2 The Oil Crew finished third or second.
:- assign("Oil Crew", _, R), R != 2, R != 3.

% 4.3 The orange team finished third or second.
:- assign(T, "orange", R), T != "Oil Crew", R != 2, R != 3.
```

```asp
% Clue 2: Verinya was discovered in 1761.
discover("Verinya", 1761, C) :- culture(C).

% Clue 3: Jujihm was discovered 14 years after the island on which the Wainani people lived.
% So the Wainani island was discovered in 1754.
discover(I, 1754, "Wainani") :- islands(I).

% So Fushil must be 1754 → Kukani in 1761 → Verinya has culture Kukani
discover("Fushil", 1754, C) :- culture(C).
```

```asp
:- play("Charles", G, _), G != 9, G != 10.
```

```asp
days("May 10th"; "May 11th"; "May 12th"; "May 13th").
topics("global warming"; "nitrogen usage"; "sulfur oxide"; "wind power").

% Each presenter gives exactly one talk on one day and one topic.
```

```asp
% Each day has exactly one talk.
1 { talk(P, D, T) : presenters(P), topics(T) } 1 :- days(D).

% Each topic is used exactly once.
1 { talk(P, D, T) : presenters(P), days(D) } 1 :- topics(T).

% 3. Inez will discuss global warming.
talk("Inez", D, "global warming") :- days(D).

% Define the immediate next day relation
day_after("May 10th", "May 11th").
day_after("May 11th", "May 12th").
day_after("May 12th", "May 13th").

% 1. The sulfur oxide expert is scheduled 1 day after Gerard.
:- talk("Gerard", D1, _), talk(_, D2, "sulfur oxide"), not day_after(D1, D2).

% 2. The nitrogen usage expert is scheduled 1 day before Alexander.
:- talk(_, D1, "nitrogen usage"), talk("Alexander", D2, _), not day_after(D1, D2).

% 4. Mable is scheduled 2 days after the sulfur oxide expert.
% Valid pairs: (May 10th, May 12th), (May 11th, May 13th)
% Use a helper predicate to define "two days after"
two_days_after(D1, D2) :- day_after(D1, D3), day_after(D3, D2).

% Apply constraint: Mable is two days after sulfur oxide expert
:- talk(_, D1, "sulfur oxide"), talk("Mable", D2, _), not two_days_after(D1, D2).
```

```asp
% Constants
players("Charles"; "Evan"; "Karl"; "Vincent").
games(8; 9; 10; 11).
positions("center field"; "first base"; "shortstop"; "third base").

% Each player has exactly one game count and one position
{ play(P, G, Pos) } :- players(P), games(G), positions(Pos).

% Each game count is used exactly once
:- #count{ P, Pos : play(P, G, Pos) } != 1, games(G).

% Each position is used exactly once
:- #count{ P, G : play(P, G, Pos) } != 1, positions(Pos).

% Each player has exactly one game count and one position
:- #count{ G, Pos : play(P, G, Pos) } != 1, players(P).

% 1. Evan played 9 games.
play("Evan", 9, Pos) :- positions(Pos).

% 2. Vincent played first base.
play("Vincent", G, "first base") :- games(G).

% 3. The player who played third base and the player who played center field are different.
:- play(P, _, "third base"), play(P, _, "center field").

% 4. The player who played third base played 2 more games than the player who played center field.
:- play(P1, G1, "third base"), play(P2, G2, "center field"), G1 != G2 + 2.

% 5. Charles and the player who played center field are different.
:- play("Charles", _, _), play("Charles", _, "center field").

% 6. Charles played 2 more games than the player who played center field.
:- play("Charles", Gc, _), play(P, Gcf, "center field"), Gc != Gcf + 2.

% 7. The boy who played 9 games and the person who played 10 games are different.
:- play(P1, 9, _), play(P2, 10, _), P1 == P2.

% 8. Charles is either the boy who played 9 games or the person who played 10 games.
% This means: Charles has either 9 or 10 games, and the 9-game and 10-game players are different.
% Enforce that Charles has 9 or 10 games.
:- play("Charles", G, _), G != 9, G != 10.
```
